fr.elements: Main Building; Front Driveway; Landscaped Area; Open Green Space; Perimeter Fencing
fr.considerations: Single Access Point; Dense Vegetation; Limited Rear Access
fr.recommendations: Enhancing emergency response effectiveness on the property could be achieved by addressing the current access and visibility conditions. Expanding or adding additional access points may enhance the ability of responders to reach critical areas quickly. Managing the dense vegetation, particularly near the driveway and around access points, could improve visibility and provide clearer pathways for emergency movement. Consideration could also be given to the creation of additional access routes or clearance zones toward the rear of the property to address the limited rear access. Integrating these measures with efforts to maintain unobstructed views and open spaces throughout could assist in staging operations and facilitate smoother navigation for response teams.

wildfire.conditions: Tree Cluster; Dense Shrub Cover; Vegetated Perimeter; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Scattered Trees; Open Terrain; Defensible Space; Vegetated Access Route; Continuous Shrub Cover; Open Green Space
wildfire.recommendations: Enhancing wildfire resilience on the property could involve reducing the dense shrub cover and increasing separation between tree clusters and scattered trees to break up fuel continuity. Managing vegetation adjacent to the structure by creating a buffer zone with less combustible materials might reduce potential exposure to heat and flames. Additionally, maintaining the open green spaces and the existing defensible space can further support suppression efforts and mitigate fire spread. Improving the vegetated access route by trimming or removing some of the vegetation could support firefighter ingress and egress. Overall, this approach to vegetation management would aim to enhance the property’s defensible space while supporting long-term wildfire preparedness and resilience.
